  • Avoids duplicate handling.
  • Allows centralized exception management.
  • Improves code maintainability.

4. Real Project Usage

In Spring Boot, service-layer exceptions are often propagated to the controller layer, where a global exception handler converts them into appropriate HTTP responses.

5. Interview Tips

  • Unchecked exceptions propagate automatically.
  • Checked exceptions must be caught or declared using throws.

6. Follow-up Questions

  • Can checked exceptions propagate?
  • How does Spring Boot handle propagated exceptions?

Interview Answer (40 sec)

Exception propagation is the process of passing an exception from one method to its caller until it is handled. This enables centralized exception handling and reduces duplicate code. Checked exceptions must be declared or caught, while unchecked exceptions propagate automatically.

subtopics.name: Suppressed Exceptions
subtopics.content:
1. What are Suppressed Exceptions?

Suppressed exceptions occur when one exception is thrown in the try block and another exception occurs while closing a resource. Java preserves the secondary exception as a suppressed exception instead of discarding it.

2. Why were they introduced?

Before Java 7, exceptions thrown while closing resources could hide the original exception. Try-with-resources automatically preserves both exceptions.

3. Example

try (BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new FileReader("file.txt"))) {
    throw new RuntimeException("Main Exception");
}

If closing the BufferedReader also throws an exception, it becomes a suppressed exception.

4. How to Access?

catch (Exception e) {
    Throwable[] suppressed = e.getSuppressed();
}

5. Real Project Usage

Suppressed exceptions are primarily encountered when using try-with-resources to manage files, database connections, sockets, and streams.

6. Interview Tips

  • Suppressed exceptions are mainly associated with try-with-resources.
  • Use getSuppressed() to retrieve them.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • What is try-with-resources?
  • How does AutoCloseable work?

Interview Answer (45 sec)

Suppressed exceptions occur when an exception is thrown while another exception is already being processed, typically during resource cleanup in try-with-resources. Java preserves these secondary exceptions instead of losing them, and they can be accessed using the getSuppressed() method.

subtopics.name: JVM, JDK, JRE
subtopics.content:
1. What is JDK?

JDK (Java Development Kit) is a complete development package used to develop, compile, debug, and run Java applications.

  • Contains JRE.
  • Contains development tools like javac, javadoc, jdb, and jar.

2. What is JRE?

JRE (Java Runtime Environment) provides the environment required to run Java applications.

  • Contains JVM.
  • Contains Java libraries and runtime files.

3. What is JVM?

JVM (Java Virtual Machine) is the runtime engine responsible for loading, verifying, and executing Java bytecode. It also manages memory, garbage collection, and security.

4. Relationship

JDK
 ├── JRE
 │    ├── JVM

5. Responsibilities of JVM

  • Class Loading
  • Bytecode Verification
  • Memory Management
  • Garbage Collection
  • JIT Compilation

6. Real Project Usage

Developers use the JDK to build applications, while production servers require only the JRE (or the appropriate Java runtime). The JVM executes Spring Boot applications and manages memory automatically.

7. Interview Tips

  • JDK = Development.
  • JRE = Runtime.
  • JVM = Execution Engine.

8. Follow-up Questions

  • What is JIT Compiler?
  • How does JVM achieve platform independence?

Interview Answer (50 sec)

JDK is the complete Java development kit containing tools to develop and compile Java applications. JRE provides the runtime environment required to execute Java programs, while JVM is the execution engine that loads, verifies, and runs bytecode. JVM also manages memory, performs garbage collection, and enables Java's platform independence.

subtopics.name: Heap vs Stack
subtopics.content:
1. Heap Memory

Heap memory stores objects and instance variables. It is shared among all threads and managed by the Garbage Collector.

2. Stack Memory

Each thread has its own Stack memory, which stores local variables, method calls, and references to objects.

3. Comparison

FeatureHeapStack
StoresObjectsLocal Variables & Method Frames
SharedYesNo
Managed ByGarbage CollectorJVM
LifetimeUntil unreachableMethod execution

4. Real Project Usage

Large object creation affects Heap memory, while deep recursion or excessive method calls may cause StackOverflowError.

5. Interview Tips

  • Objects live in Heap.
  • Method execution happens on Stack.

6. Follow-up Questions

  • What is StackOverflowError?
  • What is OutOfMemoryError?

Interview Answer (45 sec)

Heap stores objects and is shared across all threads, while Stack stores local variables and method frames for each thread. Heap is managed by the Garbage Collector, whereas Stack memory is automatically released when a method finishes execution.

subtopics.name: Memory Leaks
subtopics.content:
1. What is a Memory Leak?

A memory leak occurs when objects are no longer needed by the application but are still referenced, preventing the Garbage Collector from reclaiming their memory.

2. Common Causes

  • Static collections holding references.
  • Unclosed resources.
  • Listener or callback registrations not removed.
  • Caching objects indefinitely.
  • ThreadLocal variables not cleared.

3. Symptoms

  • Increasing Heap usage.
  • Frequent Garbage Collection.
  • OutOfMemoryError.
  • Poor application performance.

4. Detection Tools

  • VisualVM
  • Java Flight Recorder (JFR)
  • JConsole
  • Eclipse MAT

5. Real Project Usage

Long-running Spring Boot services should avoid retaining unnecessary references in caches or static collections. Monitoring Heap usage helps detect memory leaks early.

6. Interview Tips

  • Java can still have memory leaks despite Garbage Collection.
  • Leaks occur due to live references, not because GC fails.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • How do you identify a memory leak?
  • Can Garbage Collection prevent all leaks?

Interview Answer (50 sec)

A memory leak occurs when objects remain reachable through references even though the application no longer needs them. Because the Garbage Collector only removes unreachable objects, these retained references cause increasing Heap usage and can eventually lead to OutOfMemoryError. Common causes include static collections, improper caching, and unclosed resources.

subtopics.name: Garbage Collection
subtopics.content:
1. What is Garbage Collection?

Garbage Collection (GC) is the JVM process of automatically identifying and removing objects that are no longer reachable, freeing Heap memory.

2. Advantages

  • Automatic memory management.
  • Prevents manual memory deallocation.
  • Reduces memory-related bugs.

3. Popular Garbage Collectors

  • Serial GC
  • Parallel GC
  • G1 GC (Default in modern JDKs)
  • ZGC
  • Shenandoah GC

4. How GC Works

  1. Marks reachable objects.
  2. Removes unreachable objects.
  3. Compacts memory (collector-dependent).

5. Real Project Usage

Spring Boot applications rely on the JVM's Garbage Collector to manage object lifecycles. GC logs and monitoring tools are used to identify memory pressure and optimize performance.

6. Interview Tips

  • GC works only on Heap memory.
  • GC is automatic but not guaranteed to run immediately after System.gc().

7. Follow-up Questions

  • What is G1 GC?
  • What is Stop-The-World?

Interview Answer (45 sec)

Garbage Collection is the JVM's automatic memory management process that removes unreachable objects from Heap memory. It improves application reliability by eliminating manual memory management. Modern JVMs use collectors such as G1 GC, ZGC, and Shenandoah to balance throughput and pause times.

subtopics.name: Young vs Old Generation
subtopics.content:
1. JVM Heap Generations

The JVM divides Heap memory into generations to optimize Garbage Collection.

2. Young Generation

  • Stores newly created objects.
  • Contains Eden Space and Survivor Spaces (S0 & S1).
  • Minor GC occurs here frequently.

3. Old Generation

  • Stores long-lived objects promoted from the Young Generation.
  • Major GC or Full GC occurs here.

4. Lifecycle

New Object
     ↓
Eden
     ↓
Survivor (S0/S1)
     ↓
Old Generation

5. Why use Generations?

Most objects die young. Separating short-lived and long-lived objects improves GC efficiency and reduces pause times.

6. Real Project Usage

Applications creating many temporary objects, such as REST APIs and JSON processing, generate frequent Minor GCs. Monitoring promotion rates helps identify memory tuning opportunities.

7. Interview Tips

  • Minor GC = Young Generation.
  • Major/Full GC = Old Generation.

8. Follow-up Questions

  • What is Eden Space?
  • What are Survivor Spaces?
  • What is object promotion?

Interview Answer (50 sec)

The JVM divides Heap memory into Young and Old Generations. New objects are created in the Young Generation, which consists of Eden and Survivor spaces. Objects that survive multiple garbage collection cycles are promoted to the Old Generation. This generational approach improves performance because most objects are short-lived and can be collected quickly using Minor GC.

subtopics.name: Metaspace
subtopics.content:
1. What is Metaspace?

Metaspace is the memory area introduced in Java 8 to store class metadata. It replaced the Permanent Generation (PermGen) used in earlier Java versions.

2. What does Metaspace store?

  • Class metadata
  • Method metadata
  • Runtime constant pool
  • Annotations
  • ClassLoader information

3. Why was PermGen replaced?

PermGen had a fixed size, which often caused OutOfMemoryError: PermGen Space. Metaspace uses native memory and can grow dynamically, reducing these issues.

4. Advantages

  • Uses native memory.
  • Dynamically grows when required.
  • Reduces OutOfMemoryError caused by PermGen.

5. Real Project Usage

Applications that dynamically load classes, such as Spring Boot applications using proxies or plugins, consume Metaspace. Excessive dynamic class loading without proper cleanup can still cause Metaspace-related memory issues.

6. Interview Tips

  • PermGen was removed in Java 8.
  • Metaspace uses native memory instead of Heap.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • Can Metaspace cause OutOfMemoryError?
  • What JVM options configure Metaspace?

Interview Answer (45 sec)

Metaspace is the memory area introduced in Java 8 to store class metadata. It replaced PermGen and uses native memory instead of Heap, allowing it to grow dynamically. This reduces memory issues associated with the fixed-size PermGen while improving flexibility for applications that load many classes.

subtopics.name: ClassLoader
subtopics.content:
1. What is ClassLoader?

ClassLoader is a JVM component responsible for loading Java classes into memory at runtime. It loads .class files only when they are required.

2. Types of ClassLoaders

  • Bootstrap ClassLoader - Loads core Java classes (java.lang, java.util, etc.).
  • Platform (Extension) ClassLoader - Loads platform libraries.
  • Application ClassLoader - Loads classes from the application's classpath.

3. Parent Delegation Model

When a class needs to be loaded, the request is first delegated to the parent ClassLoader. Only if the parent cannot load the class does the child attempt to load it.

4. Why Parent Delegation?

  • Prevents duplicate class loading.
  • Improves security.
  • Ensures core Java classes cannot be overridden.

5. Real Project Usage

Spring Boot, application servers, and plugin-based applications use multiple ClassLoaders to isolate applications and dynamically load components.

6. Interview Tips

  • Remember the order: Bootstrap → Platform → Application.
  • Mention Parent Delegation Model.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • Can we create a custom ClassLoader?
  • Why can't we override java.lang.String?

Interview Answer (50 sec)

ClassLoader is responsible for loading Java classes into memory when required. Java uses three main ClassLoaders: Bootstrap, Platform, and Application ClassLoader. They follow the Parent Delegation Model, which improves security and prevents duplicate loading of core Java classes.

subtopics.name: Reference Types
subtopics.content:
1. Types of References in Java

Java provides four types of references that determine how the Garbage Collector treats objects.

2. Strong Reference

The default reference type. Objects with strong references are never garbage collected while the reference exists.

3. Weak Reference

Objects are eligible for Garbage Collection as soon as they become weakly reachable. Used by WeakHashMap.

4. Soft Reference

Objects are retained until the JVM requires memory. Useful for implementing caches.

5. Phantom Reference

Objects are already finalized and are about to be reclaimed. Mainly used for advanced resource cleanup and memory management.

6. Comparison

ReferenceGC Behavior
StrongNever collected while referenced
SoftCollected only when memory is low
WeakCollected during next GC
PhantomCollected after cleanup notification

7. Real Project Usage

Soft references are useful for memory-sensitive caches, while WeakHashMap uses weak references to automatically remove entries whose keys are no longer referenced.

8. Interview Tips

  • Strong is the default reference.
  • WeakHashMap uses Weak References.

9. Follow-up Questions

  • Difference between SoftReference and WeakReference?
  • What is ReferenceQueue?

Interview Answer (55 sec)

Java supports Strong, Soft, Weak, and Phantom references. Strong references prevent objects from being garbage collected. Soft references are retained until memory becomes scarce, making them suitable for caches. Weak references are collected during the next garbage collection cycle and are used by WeakHashMap. Phantom references are used for advanced cleanup after an object becomes unreachable.

subtopics.name: Finalize Deprecation
subtopics.content:
1. What is finalize()?

The finalize() method was provided by the Object class to perform cleanup before an object was garbage collected.

2. Why was it deprecated?

  • Unpredictable execution.
  • No guarantee that it will run.
  • Poor performance.
  • Security concerns.
  • Could delay Garbage Collection.

3. Java Recommendation

Since Java 9, finalize() has been deprecated. Developers should use try-with-resources, AutoCloseable, or Cleaner API instead.

4. Alternatives

  • try-with-resources
  • AutoCloseable
  • Cleaner API

5. Real Project Usage

Modern Spring Boot applications avoid finalize(). Resources such as database connections, files, and sockets are managed using try-with-resources or framework-managed lifecycle methods.

6. Interview Tips

  • Do not recommend finalize().
  • Mention Cleaner API as the modern replacement.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • What replaced finalize()?
  • Why is finalize() unreliable?

Interview Answer (40 sec)

The finalize() method was originally intended for cleanup before an object was garbage collected, but it has been deprecated because its execution is unpredictable and can negatively affect performance. Modern Java recommends using try-with-resources, AutoCloseable, or the Cleaner API instead.

subtopics.name: JVM Tuning Basics
subtopics.content:
1. What is JVM Tuning?

JVM tuning is the process of configuring JVM memory and Garbage Collection settings to improve application performance, reduce pause times, and optimize resource utilization.

2. Common JVM Parameters

OptionPurpose
-XmsInitial Heap Size
-XmxMaximum Heap Size
-XX:MaxMetaspaceSizeMaximum Metaspace Size
-XX:+UseG1GCEnable G1 Garbage Collector

3. Monitoring Tools

  • JVisualVM
  • Java Flight Recorder (JFR)
  • JConsole
  • GC Logs

4. Best Practices

  • Monitor Heap usage regularly.
  • Avoid excessive Full GC.
  • Choose an appropriate Garbage Collector.
  • Size Heap based on application workload.

5. Real Project Usage

In production Spring Boot applications, JVM tuning often involves adjusting Heap size, selecting G1 GC, enabling GC logs, and monitoring memory usage to reduce latency and improve throughput.

6. Interview Tips

  • Do not recommend tuning without monitoring.
  • Always mention GC logs and profiling tools.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • When would you increase -Xmx?
  • How do you identify GC issues?
  • Why is G1 GC the default?

Interview Answer (60 sec)

JVM tuning involves optimizing Heap size, Metaspace, and Garbage Collection settings to improve application performance. Common parameters include -Xms for initial Heap size, -Xmx for maximum Heap size, and G1 GC for efficient memory management. Before tuning, I always monitor the application using tools like VisualVM, Java Flight Recorder, and GC logs to identify actual bottlenecks.

subtopics.name: Lambda Expressions
subtopics.content:
1. What are Lambda Expressions?

Lambda Expressions were introduced in Java 8 to provide a concise way of implementing functional interfaces without creating anonymous inner classes.

2. Syntax

(parameters) -> expression

(parameters) -> {
    // multiple statements
}

3. Example

// Before Java 8
Runnable r = new Runnable() {
    @Override
    public void run() {
        System.out.println("Hello");
    }
};

// Java 8
Runnable r = () -> System.out.println("Hello");

4. Advantages

  • Less boilerplate code.
  • Improves readability.
  • Works seamlessly with Streams API.
  • Encourages functional programming.

5. Real Project Usage

Lambda expressions are extensively used in Spring Boot applications with Streams, collection processing, sorting, asynchronous programming, and functional interfaces such as Predicate and Consumer.

6. Interview Tips

  • Lambda works only with Functional Interfaces.
  • It replaces anonymous inner classes in many scenarios.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • Can Lambda access local variables?
  • What is an effectively final variable?

Interview Answer (45 sec)

Lambda expressions, introduced in Java 8, provide a concise way to implement functional interfaces. They reduce boilerplate code by replacing anonymous inner classes and are widely used with Streams, collection processing, and asynchronous programming. A lambda can only be used with a functional interface, which contains exactly one abstract method.

subtopics.name: Functional Interfaces
subtopics.content:
1. What is a Functional Interface?

A Functional Interface is an interface that contains exactly one abstract method. It can have multiple default and static methods.

2. @FunctionalInterface

The @FunctionalInterface annotation ensures that the interface contains only one abstract method.

3. Example

@FunctionalInterface
interface Calculator {
    int add(int a, int b);
}

4. Common Functional Interfaces

  • Predicate
  • Function
  • Consumer
  • Supplier
  • Runnable
  • Callable
  • Comparator

5. Real Project Usage

Functional interfaces are widely used with Lambda expressions, Streams API, asynchronous programming, event handling, and callback implementations.

6. Interview Tips

  • Exactly one abstract method.
  • Default and static methods are allowed.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • Can a functional interface have multiple default methods?
  • Is Runnable a functional interface?

Interview Answer (45 sec)

A Functional Interface contains exactly one abstract method and serves as the target type for lambda expressions. It may also contain any number of default and static methods. Common examples include Predicate, Function, Consumer, Supplier, Runnable, and Comparator.

subtopics.name: Predicate, Function, Consumer, Supplier
subtopics.content:
1. Predicate<T>

Represents a condition and returns a boolean.

Predicate<Integer> even = n -> n % 2 == 0;

2. Function<T,R>

Takes one input and returns one output.

Function<String,Integer> length = s -> s.length();

3. Consumer<T>

Consumes input and returns nothing.

Consumer<String> print = System.out::println;

4. Supplier<T>

Supplies data without taking any input.

Supplier<UUID> supplier = UUID::randomUUID;

5. Comparison

InterfaceInputOutput
PredicateYesBoolean
FunctionYesOne Result
ConsumerYesNone
SupplierNoOne Result

6. Real Project Usage

Predicate is commonly used for filtering, Function for object transformation, Consumer for logging or processing, and Supplier for lazy initialization and object creation.

7. Interview Tips

  • Predicate returns boolean.
  • Consumer returns void.
  • Supplier takes no input.

8. Follow-up Questions

  • What are BiFunction and BiConsumer?
  • How are these used in Streams?

Interview Answer (60 sec)

Predicate evaluates a condition and returns a boolean. Function transforms one object into another. Consumer accepts an input and performs an operation without returning a value. Supplier provides data without taking any input. These interfaces are heavily used with Lambda expressions and the Streams API.

subtopics.name: Streams API
subtopics.content:
1. What is Streams API?

Streams API, introduced in Java 8, provides a functional approach to processing collections without modifying the original data.

2. Stream Pipeline

Source → Intermediate Operations → Terminal Operation

3. Common Intermediate Operations

  • filter()
  • map()
  • sorted()
  • distinct()
  • limit()

4. Common Terminal Operations

  • collect()
  • forEach()
  • count()
  • reduce()
  • findFirst()

5. Example

List<String> names = Arrays.asList("A","B","C");
List<String> result = names.stream()
    .filter(s -> !s.isEmpty())
    .collect(Collectors.toList());

6. Real Project Usage

Streams are widely used for filtering database results, DTO transformations, grouping records, aggregations, sorting, and processing REST API responses.

7. Interview Tips

  • Streams do not modify the original collection.
  • Intermediate operations are lazy.

8. Follow-up Questions

  • What is lazy evaluation?
  • Difference between Collection and Stream?

Interview Answer (55 sec)

Streams API provides a functional and declarative way to process collections. A stream pipeline consists of a source, intermediate operations such as filter() and map(), and a terminal operation like collect() or reduce(). Streams improve code readability and are heavily used for filtering, transformation, sorting, and aggregation.

subtopics.name: map vs flatMap
subtopics.content:
1. map()

map() transforms each element into another object while maintaining a one-to-one relationship.

List<String> names = Arrays.asList("java", "spring");
List<String> upper = names.stream()
    .map(String::toUpperCase)
    .collect(Collectors.toList());

2. flatMap()

flatMap() transforms each element into a Stream and then flattens all Streams into a single Stream.

List<List<String>> list = ...;
list.stream()
    .flatMap(Collection::stream)
    .collect(Collectors.toList());

3. Comparison

Featuremap()flatMap()
TransformationOne-to-OneOne-to-Many
ReturnsObjectFlattened Stream
Nested CollectionsNoYes

4. Real Project Usage

map() is commonly used for DTO conversion, while flatMap() is used for flattening nested collections such as lists of orders containing lists of products.

5. Interview Tips

  • map() transforms.
  • flatMap() transforms and flattens.

6. Follow-up Questions

  • Can flatMap() replace map()?
  • What does Collection::stream do?

Interview Answer (50 sec)

map() performs a one-to-one transformation, converting each element into another object. flatMap() is used when each element itself contains multiple values, such as a collection or stream. It converts each element into a stream and then flattens all streams into a single stream. I use map() for DTO transformations and flatMap() for nested collections.

subtopics.name: filter vs map
subtopics.content:
1. filter()

filter() is used to select elements from a Stream based on a condition. It returns only the elements that satisfy the given Predicate.

List<Integer> numbers = Arrays.asList(1,2,3,4,5);
List<Integer> even = numbers.stream()
    .filter(n -> n % 2 == 0)
    .collect(Collectors.toList());

2. map()

map() transforms each element into another object without changing the number of elements.

List<String> names = Arrays.asList("java", "spring");
List<String> upper = names.stream()
    .map(String::toUpperCase)
    .collect(Collectors.toList());

3. Comparison

Featurefilter()map()
PurposeSelect ElementsTransform Elements
InputPredicateFunction
Output SizeMay DecreaseUsually Same

4. Real Project Usage

filter() is commonly used to select active users, valid records, or specific orders. map() is widely used for DTO conversion, formatting data, and transforming entities into API responses.

5. Interview Tips

  • filter() removes unwanted elements.
  • map() converts elements into another form.

6. Follow-up Questions

  • Can filter() be used after map()?
  • Difference between map() and flatMap()?

Interview Answer (45 sec)

filter() is used to select elements that satisfy a condition, while map() transforms each element into another object. filter() can reduce the number of elements in the stream, whereas map() generally preserves the number of elements by converting them into a different representation.

subtopics.name: reduce()
subtopics.content:
1. What is reduce()?

reduce() is a terminal Stream operation used to combine all elements into a single result, such as a sum, product, maximum, minimum, or concatenated string.

2. Example

List<Integer> numbers = Arrays.asList(1,2,3,4,5);
int sum = numbers.stream()
    .reduce(0, Integer::sum);

Output: 15

3. Syntax

stream.reduce(identity, accumulator)
  • Identity - Initial value.
  • Accumulator - Combines previous result with the next element.

4. Common Uses

  • Sum
  • Maximum
  • Minimum
  • String concatenation
  • Custom aggregations

5. Real Project Usage

reduce() is frequently used for calculating total order amounts, invoice totals, salaries, discounts, and other aggregated business values.

6. Interview Tips

  • reduce() returns a single value.
  • It is a terminal operation.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • Difference between reduce() and collect()?
  • Can reduce() return Optional?

Interview Answer (45 sec)

reduce() is a terminal Stream operation used to combine multiple elements into a single result. It is commonly used for operations like calculating sums, maximum values, minimum values, and custom aggregations. It takes an identity value and an accumulator function that combines the elements.

subtopics.name: Optional
subtopics.content:
1. What is Optional?

Optional is a container object introduced in Java 8 to represent the presence or absence of a value. It helps reduce NullPointerException by encouraging explicit handling of missing values.

2. Creating Optional

Optional.of(value)
Optional.ofNullable(value)
Optional.empty()

3. Common Methods

  • isPresent()
  • ifPresent()
  • orElse()
  • orElseGet()
  • orElseThrow()
  • map()
  • filter()

4. Example

Optional<String> name = Optional.ofNullable(user.getName());
String result = name.orElse("Unknown");

5. Real Project Usage

Optional is commonly used in Spring Data JPA repository methods such as findById() to indicate that a result may or may not exist. It is also useful when dealing with optional configuration values or API responses.

6. Interview Tips

  • Avoid calling get() without checking.
  • Prefer orElseThrow() for mandatory values.
  • Avoid using Optional for entity fields or method parameters.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • Difference between orElse() and orElseGet()?
  • Should Optional be used in DTOs?

Interview Answer (55 sec)

Optional is a container introduced in Java 8 to represent a value that may or may not be present. It helps avoid NullPointerException by forcing developers to explicitly handle missing values using methods like orElse(), orElseThrow(), and ifPresent(). In Spring Boot, Optional is commonly returned by repository methods such as findById().

subtopics.name: Method References
subtopics.content:
1. What are Method References?

Method References provide a shorter and cleaner way to refer to an existing method using the :: operator. They are a shorthand form of lambda expressions.

2. Types of Method References

  • Static Method Reference
  • Instance Method of a Particular Object
  • Instance Method of an Arbitrary Object
  • Constructor Reference

3. Examples

System.out::println
String::toUpperCase
Integer::parseInt
ArrayList::new

4. Advantages

  • Cleaner syntax.
  • Improves readability.
  • Avoids unnecessary lambda expressions.

5. Real Project Usage

Method references are frequently used with Streams API for printing, mapping, sorting, parsing values, collecting results, and object creation.

6. Interview Tips

  • Method references can only be used when a lambda simply calls an existing method.
  • Uses the :: operator.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • Difference between Lambda and Method Reference?
  • What is a Constructor Reference?

Interview Answer (45 sec)

Method References are a shorthand form of lambda expressions that refer directly to existing methods using the :: operator. They improve readability and reduce boilerplate code. Common examples include System.out::println, String::toUpperCase, and ArrayList::new.

subtopics.name: Collectors
subtopics.content:
1. What are Collectors?

Collectors are utility methods provided by the Collectors class to collect Stream elements into various data structures or perform aggregations.

2. Common Collectors

  • toList()
  • toSet()
  • toMap()
  • joining()
  • groupingBy()
  • partitioningBy()
  • counting()
  • mapping()
  • summarizingInt()

3. Example

Map<String, List<Employee>> employeesByDept = employees.stream()
    .collect(Collectors.groupingBy(Employee::getDepartment));

4. Common Use Cases

  • Grouping records
  • Creating Maps
  • Removing duplicates
  • Calculating statistics
  • Joining Strings

5. Real Project Usage

Collectors are extensively used in enterprise applications to group orders by customer, calculate sales reports, generate dashboards, convert lists into maps, and prepare API responses.

6. Interview Tips

  • groupingBy() is one of the most frequently asked collectors.
  • Know the difference between groupingBy() and partitioningBy().

7. Follow-up Questions

  • Difference between groupingBy() and partitioningBy()?
  • When would you use toMap()?

Interview Answer (60 sec)

Collectors are utility methods used to collect Stream results into collections or perform aggregations. Common collectors include toList(), toSet(), toMap(), groupingBy(), partitioningBy(), and joining(). In enterprise applications, I frequently use groupingBy() for reporting, toMap() for lookups, and joining() for string aggregation.

subtopics.name: Process vs Thread
subtopics.content:
1. What is a Process?

A Process is an independent program in execution. Each process has its own memory space, resources, and execution environment.

2. What is a Thread?

A Thread is the smallest unit of execution within a process. Multiple threads share the same process memory but execute independently.

3. Comparison

FeatureProcessThread
MemorySeparateShared
CommunicationInter-Process Communication (IPC)Shared Memory
Creation CostHighLow
Failure ImpactUsually isolatedMay affect entire process

4. Real Project Usage

A Spring Boot application typically runs as a single process with multiple threads handling incoming HTTP requests simultaneously.

5. Interview Tips

  • Threads share Heap memory.
  • Each thread has its own Stack.

6. Follow-up Questions

  • Can threads communicate faster than processes?
  • Why are threads considered lightweight?

Interview Answer (50 sec)

A process is an independent program with its own memory space and system resources. A thread is a lightweight unit of execution within a process. Multiple threads share the same Heap memory but have separate Stack memory. In Spring Boot, a single application process usually handles multiple client requests using different threads.

subtopics.name: Ways to Create Threads
subtopics.content:
1. Extending Thread Class

class MyThread extends Thread {
    public void run() {
        System.out.println("Running...");
    }
}

2. Implementing Runnable

class MyTask implements Runnable {
    public void run() {
        System.out.println("Running...");
    }
}

3. Implementing Callable

Callable<Integer> task = () -> 100;
Future<Integer> future = executor.submit(task);

4. Which approach is preferred?

In modern Java, Runnable or Callable with ExecutorService is preferred because it supports thread pooling, better resource management, and result handling.

5. Real Project Usage

Spring Boot applications rarely create threads manually. Instead, they use ExecutorService, CompletableFuture, or Spring's @Async support.

6. Interview Tips

  • Avoid extending Thread unless necessary.
  • Prefer ExecutorService in production applications.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • Why is ExecutorService preferred?
  • Difference between Runnable and Callable?

Interview Answer (45 sec)

Threads can be created by extending the Thread class, implementing Runnable, or implementing Callable. In enterprise applications, Runnable or Callable together with ExecutorService is preferred because it supports thread pools, efficient resource management, and asynchronous execution.

subtopics.name: Runnable vs Callable
subtopics.content:
1. Runnable

Runnable represents a task that does not return a result and cannot throw checked exceptions.

Runnable task = () -> System.out.println("Hello");

2. Callable

Callable represents a task that returns a result and can throw checked exceptions.

Callable<Integer> task = () -> 100;

3. Comparison

FeatureRunnableCallable
Return ValueNoYes
Checked ExceptionsNoYes
Submit ToThread / ExecutorServiceExecutorService
ResultNoneFuture<T>

4. Real Project Usage

Runnable is suitable for background tasks such as logging, while Callable is used for asynchronous operations that return results, such as fetching data from multiple services.

5. Interview Tips

  • Callable returns Future.
  • Runnable cannot return values.

6. Follow-up Questions

  • What is Future?
  • Can Runnable throw RuntimeException?

Interview Answer (45 sec)

Runnable is used for tasks that do not return a result and cannot throw checked exceptions. Callable can return a value and throw checked exceptions, with the result accessed through a Future. In enterprise applications, Callable is commonly used for asynchronous service calls and parallel processing.

subtopics.name: synchronized Keyword
subtopics.content:
1. What is synchronized?

The synchronized keyword ensures that only one thread can execute a synchronized block or method on the same object at a time.

2. Types

  • Synchronized Method
  • Synchronized Block
  • Static Synchronized Method (locks the Class object)

3. Example

public synchronized void increment() {
    count++;
}

4. Why use synchronized?

  • Prevents race conditions.
  • Ensures thread safety.
  • Protects shared resources.

5. Real Project Usage

Synchronization is useful when multiple threads modify shared data. However, excessive synchronization can reduce performance, so concurrent utilities like AtomicInteger or ConcurrentHashMap are often preferred.

6. Interview Tips

  • Synchronization provides mutual exclusion.
  • Every object has an intrinsic lock (monitor).

7. Follow-up Questions

  • Difference between synchronized method and block?
  • What is an intrinsic lock?

Interview Answer (50 sec)

The synchronized keyword ensures that only one thread can execute a critical section at a time by acquiring the object's monitor lock. It is used to protect shared resources from race conditions. While synchronization ensures thread safety, it should be used carefully because excessive locking can reduce performance.

subtopics.name: volatile
subtopics.content:
1. What is volatile?

The volatile keyword ensures that changes made to a variable by one thread are immediately visible to all other threads.

2. What does volatile guarantee?

  • Visibility of changes across threads.
  • Prevents instruction reordering around the volatile variable.

3. What does volatile NOT guarantee?

  • Atomicity.
  • Mutual exclusion.

4. Example

private volatile boolean running = true;

If one thread changes running to false, all other threads immediately observe the updated value.

5. volatile vs synchronized

Featurevolatilesynchronized
VisibilityYesYes
AtomicityNoYes
LockingNoYes

6. Real Project Usage

volatile is commonly used for status flags, shutdown signals, and configuration values that are read by multiple threads but do not require compound operations.

7. Interview Tips

  • volatile is not a replacement for synchronized.
  • count++ is not thread-safe even if count is volatile.

8. Follow-up Questions

  • Why is count++ not atomic?
  • When should you choose AtomicInteger instead?

Interview Answer (50 sec)

The volatile keyword guarantees visibility of variable updates across threads and prevents certain instruction reorderings. However, it does not provide atomicity or locking. It is suitable for simple shared flags but not for compound operations such as incrementing a counter, where AtomicInteger or synchronized should be used.

subtopics.name: Race Condition
subtopics.content:
1. What is a Race Condition?

A race condition occurs when multiple threads access and modify shared data simultaneously, causing the final result to depend on the order of execution.

2. Example

count++;

If two threads execute this statement simultaneously, one update may be lost because increment consists of three operations: read, increment, and write.

3. Why does it happen?

  • Shared mutable data.
  • Lack of synchronization.
  • Non-atomic operations.

4. How to prevent it?

  • synchronized
  • AtomicInteger
  • Locks (ReentrantLock)
  • Concurrent Collections

5. Real Project Usage

Race conditions commonly occur when multiple threads update shared counters, caches, account balances, or inventory simultaneously.

6. Interview Tips

  • count++ is not atomic.
  • Visibility and atomicity are different concepts.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • How does synchronized prevent race conditions?
  • Why is AtomicInteger thread-safe?

Interview Answer (45 sec)

A race condition occurs when multiple threads access and modify shared data at the same time without proper synchronization. Since operations like count++ are not atomic, updates can be lost. Race conditions can be prevented using synchronized blocks, AtomicInteger, locks, or concurrent collections depending on the use case.

subtopics.name: Deadlock
subtopics.content:
1. What is Deadlock?

Deadlock is a situation where two or more threads wait indefinitely for each other to release resources, causing none of them to proceed.

2. Example

Thread 1 locks Object A and waits for Object B.
Thread 2 locks Object B and waits for Object A.

3. Four Necessary Conditions

  • Mutual Exclusion
  • Hold and Wait
  • No Preemption
  • Circular Wait

4. How to Prevent Deadlock?

  • Acquire locks in a fixed order.
  • Use lock timeouts.
  • Reduce nested synchronization.
  • Use concurrent utilities where possible.

5. Real Project Usage

Deadlocks can occur when multiple services or threads lock shared resources such as database records, files, or synchronized objects in different orders.

6. Interview Tips

  • Remember the four necessary conditions.
  • Lock ordering is the most common prevention technique.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • How do you detect deadlocks?
  • What tools can identify deadlocked threads?

Interview Answer (50 sec)

Deadlock occurs when two or more threads wait indefinitely for resources held by each other. It happens when the four necessary conditions—mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, and circular wait—are satisfied. In production systems, deadlocks are commonly avoided by acquiring locks in a consistent order and minimizing nested synchronization.

subtopics.name: ExecutorService
subtopics.content:
1. What is ExecutorService?

ExecutorService is a framework introduced in Java 5 that manages a pool of worker threads and executes submitted tasks efficiently.

2. Why use ExecutorService?

  • Thread reuse.
  • Improved performance.
  • Better resource management.
  • Supports asynchronous execution.

3. Example

ExecutorService executor = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(5);
executor.submit(() -> System.out.println("Task"));
executor.shutdown();

4. Common Factory Methods

  • newFixedThreadPool()
  • newCachedThreadPool()
  • newSingleThreadExecutor()
  • newScheduledThreadPool()

5. Real Project Usage

ExecutorService is widely used in Spring Boot applications for asynchronous processing, parallel API calls, batch jobs, and background task execution.

6. Interview Tips

  • Always shutdown the ExecutorService.
  • Avoid creating threads manually in enterprise applications.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • Difference between execute() and submit()?
  • What happens if shutdown() is not called?

Interview Answer (50 sec)

ExecutorService is a thread pool framework that manages thread creation and reuse, improving performance and resource utilization. Instead of creating threads manually, tasks are submitted to the ExecutorService, which schedules and executes them efficiently. It is widely used for asynchronous processing and concurrent task execution in enterprise applications.

subtopics.name: Future vs CompletableFuture
subtopics.content:
1. What is Future?

Future represents the result of an asynchronous computation. It allows checking whether a task has completed and retrieving its result.

2. What is CompletableFuture?

CompletableFuture extends Future by supporting asynchronous task chaining, callbacks, exception handling, and combining multiple asynchronous operations.

3. Comparison

FeatureFutureCompletableFuture
Asynchronous ResultYesYes
CallbacksNoYes
Task ChainingNoYes
Exception HandlingLimitedRich API
Combining TasksNoYes

4. Real Project Usage

CompletableFuture is widely used to call multiple microservices in parallel, improving overall response time. Future is mainly used for simple asynchronous tasks.

5. Interview Tips

  • CompletableFuture is much more powerful than Future.
  • Know methods like thenApply(), thenCompose(), thenCombine(), and exceptionally().

6. Follow-up Questions

  • Difference between thenApply() and thenCompose()?
  • How do you combine two CompletableFutures?

Interview Answer (60 sec)

Future represents the result of an asynchronous computation but provides limited functionality. CompletableFuture extends Future by supporting callbacks, task chaining, exception handling, and combining multiple asynchronous operations. In modern Spring Boot applications, CompletableFuture is preferred for parallel API calls and asynchronous workflows.

subtopics.name: AtomicInteger
subtopics.content:
1. What is AtomicInteger?

AtomicInteger is a thread-safe class in java.util.concurrent.atomic that performs atomic operations without using synchronized blocks.

2. How does it work?

AtomicInteger uses low-level CPU instructions such as Compare-And-Swap (CAS) to update values atomically.

3. Common Methods

  • incrementAndGet()
  • decrementAndGet()
  • getAndIncrement()
  • compareAndSet()
  • get()
  • set()

4. Why use AtomicInteger?

  • Thread-safe.
  • No explicit locking.
  • Better performance than synchronized for simple counters.

5. Real Project Usage

AtomicInteger is commonly used for request counters, sequence generators, metrics collection, rate limiting, and tracking concurrent operations.

6. Interview Tips

  • AtomicInteger uses CAS.
  • Suitable for simple atomic operations, not complex business logic.

7. Follow-up Questions

  • What is CAS?
  • AtomicInteger vs synchronized?

Interview Answer (50 sec)

AtomicInteger is a thread-safe class that performs atomic operations without locking by using the Compare-And-Swap (CAS) algorithm. It is commonly used for counters and sequence numbers because it offers better performance than synchronized for simple increment and update operations.

subtopics.name: Singleton Pattern
subtopics.content:
Singleton Pattern

The Singleton Pattern is a Creational Design Pattern that ensures only one instance of a class exists throughout the application and provides a global access point to that instance.

Why Do We Need Singleton?

Some resources should have only one instance in the entire application.

Creating multiple instances may lead to inconsistent behavior, unnecessary memory usage, or resource conflicts.

Examples

  • Database Connection Manager
  • Logger
  • Configuration Manager
  • Cache Manager
  • Thread Pool Manager

Imagine every service creating its own database connection manager. 


Service A -> DB Manager 1

Service B -> DB Manager 2

Service C -> DB Manager 3

This leads to wasted resources and management complexity.

Instead: 


Service A
      \
       \
Service B -----> Singleton DB Manager
       /
      /
Service C

Problem Without Singleton


DatabaseManager db1 = new DatabaseManager();

DatabaseManager db2 = new DatabaseManager();

DatabaseManager db3 = new DatabaseManager();

Now three different objects exist.

This may create:

  • Memory wastage.
  • Resource contention.
  • Data inconsistency.

Singleton Solution

The Singleton Pattern restricts object creation by:

  1. Making the constructor private.
  2. Creating a static instance.
  3. Providing a public method to access the instance.

Basic Singleton Implementation


public class Singleton {

    private static Singleton instance;

    private Singleton() {
    }

    public static Singleton getInstance() {

        if(instance == null) {
            instance = new Singleton();
        }

        return instance;
    }
}

Usage


Singleton s1 = Singleton.getInstance();

Singleton s2 = Singleton.getInstance();

System.out.println(s1 == s2);

Output: 


true

Both references point to the same object.

How It Works


First Call

getInstance()

instance == null

Create Object

Return Object

Subsequent calls: 


getInstance()

instance != null

Return Existing Object

Characteristics of Singleton

  • Only one object exists.
  • Global access point.
  • Object created on demand.
  • Controlled object lifecycle.

Eager Initialization Singleton

The object is created when the class is loaded. 


public class Singleton {

    private static final Singleton instance =
            new Singleton();

    private Singleton() {
    }

    public static Singleton getInstance() {
        return instance;
    }
}

Advantages

  • Thread safe.
  • Simple implementation.

Disadvantages

  • Object created even if not used.
  • Memory wastage possible.

Lazy Initialization Singleton

Object is created only when required. 


public class Singleton {

    private static Singleton instance;

    private Singleton() {
    }

    public static Singleton getInstance() {

        if(instance == null) {
            instance = new Singleton();
        }

        return instance;
    }
}

Advantages

  • Saves memory.
  • Object created only when needed.

Disadvantages

  • Not thread safe.

Thread Safety Problem

Suppose two threads call getInstance() simultaneously. 


Thread 1 -> instance == null

Thread 2 -> instance == null

Both threads create separate objects. 


Singleton Object 1

Singleton Object 2

Singleton guarantee is broken.

Thread Safe Singleton


public class Singleton {

    private static Singleton instance;

    private Singleton() {
    }

    public static synchronized Singleton getInstance() {

        if(instance == null) {
            instance = new Singleton();
        }

        return instance;
    }
}

Advantages

  • Thread safe.
  • Simple implementation.

Disadvantages

  • Performance overhead due to synchronization.

Double Checked Locking

This is the most common interview implementation. 


public class Singleton {

    private static volatile Singleton instance;

    private Singleton() {
    }

    public static Singleton getInstance() {

        if(instance == null) {

            synchronized (Singleton.class) {

                if(instance == null) {
                    instance = new Singleton();
                }
            }
        }

        return instance;
    }
}

Advantages

  • Thread safe.
  • High performance.
  • Object created only when required.

Best Singleton Implementation (Enum)

Joshua Bloch recommends using Enum Singleton. 


public enum Singleton {

    INSTANCE;

    public void show() {
        System.out.println("Singleton");
    }
}

Advantages

  • Thread safe.
  • Serialization safe.
  • Reflection safe.
  • Simplest implementation.

Real World Examples

Logger


Logger logger = Logger.getInstance();

All application components use the same logger instance.

Configuration Manager


ConfigManager.getInstance();

Single source of configuration data.

Cache Manager


CacheManager.getInstance();

Shared cache across the application.

Singleton in Spring Framework

By default, Spring Beans are Singleton scoped. 


@Service
public class UserService {
}

Spring creates only one instance of UserService and shares it across the application.

Advantages

  • Controlled object creation.
  • Memory efficient.
  • Global access point.
  • Useful for shared resources.
  • Easy lifecycle management.

Disadvantages

  • Acts like a global variable.
  • Difficult to unit test.
  • Can introduce hidden dependencies.
  • May violate Single Responsibility Principle.
  • Thread safety must be handled carefully.

Common Interview Questions

Why is Singleton a Creational Pattern?

Because it controls how objects are created.

How do you make Singleton thread safe?

Using synchronization, double-checked locking, or Enum.

What is the best Singleton implementation in Java?

Enum Singleton is generally considered the safest implementation.

Are Spring Beans Singleton?

Yes. Singleton is the default bean scope in Spring.

Interview Quick Notes

  • Singleton ensures only one object exists.
  • Private constructor prevents external object creation.
  • Global access is provided through getInstance().
  • Lazy initialization creates object on demand.
  • Double Checked Locking is a common production implementation.
  • Enum Singleton is considered the safest Java implementation.
  • Spring Beans are Singleton by default.

subtopics.name: Factory Method Pattern
subtopics.content:
Factory Method Pattern

The Factory Method Pattern is a Creational Design Pattern that provides an interface for creating objects while allowing subclasses to decide which specific object to instantiate.

Why Do We Need Factory Method?

Direct object creation using the new keyword tightly couples the client code with concrete classes.

Whenever a new implementation is introduced, client code must be modified. 


Payment payment = new CreditCardPayment();

If tomorrow PayPalPayment is introduced, existing code needs modification.

Factory Method solves this by moving object creation logic into a dedicated factory.

Problem Without Factory Method


if(type.equals("CREDIT_CARD")) {
    payment = new CreditCardPayment();
}
else if(type.equals("UPI")) {
    payment = new UpiPayment();
}
else if(type.equals("PAYPAL")) {
    payment = new PaypalPayment();
}

Every new payment method requires code changes.

Factory Method Solution

Instead of creating objects directly, the client asks a factory to create them. 


Client
   |
   v
Factory
   |
   +--> CreditCardPayment
   |
   +--> UpiPayment
   |
   +--> PaypalPayment

Implementation

Step 1: Create Product Interface


public interface Payment {

    void pay();
}

Step 2: Concrete Products


public class CreditCardPayment
        implements Payment {

    @Override
    public void pay() {
        System.out.println(
            "Credit Card Payment");
    }
}


public class UpiPayment
        implements Payment {

    @Override
    public void pay() {
        System.out.println(
            "UPI Payment");
    }
}

Step 3: Create Factory


public class PaymentFactory {

    public static Payment getPayment(
            String type) {

        if(type.equals("CARD")) {
            return new CreditCardPayment();
        }

        if(type.equals("UPI")) {
            return new UpiPayment();
        }

        throw new IllegalArgumentException();
    }
}

Step 4: Client Code


Payment payment =
    PaymentFactory.getPayment("UPI");

payment.pay();

How It Works

  1. Client requests an object.
  2. Factory decides which implementation to create.
  3. Factory returns the object.
  4. Client uses the interface.

Client remains unaware of concrete implementations.

Real World Example

Consider a logistics company. 


Road Transport
Air Transport
Sea Transport

The client asks for a transport service.

The factory determines which transport implementation to create.

Factory Method vs Direct Object Creation

Direct Creation Factory Method
Tightly Coupled Loosely Coupled
Hard to Extend Easy to Extend
Client Knows Class Client Knows Interface

Factory Method in Spring

Spring uses Factory Method extensively. 


@Bean
public PaymentService paymentService() {
    return new PaymentServiceImpl();
}

The framework calls the factory method to create the bean.

Advantages

  • Reduces tight coupling.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.
  • Centralized object creation.
  • Easier maintenance.
  • Improves scalability.

Disadvantages

  • Additional classes required.
  • Increased complexity for simple applications.
  • More abstraction layers.

When to Use

  • Multiple implementations exist.
  • Object creation logic is complex.
  • Client should not know concrete classes.
  • Future extensions are expected.

When Not to Use

  • Only one implementation exists.
  • Object creation is extremely simple.
  • Abstraction adds unnecessary complexity.

Common Interview Questions

What problem does Factory Method solve?

It decouples object creation from client code.

Which SOLID principle does it support?

Open/Closed Principle and Dependency Inversion Principle.

How is it different from Abstract Factory?

Factory Method creates one product type, whereas Abstract Factory creates families of related products.

Interview Quick Notes

  • Factory Method is a Creational Design Pattern.
  • Object creation is delegated to a factory.
  • Reduces tight coupling.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.
  • Client depends on abstraction, not implementation.
  • Commonly used in Spring Bean creation.

subtopics.name: Abstract Factory Pattern
subtopics.content:
Abstract Factory Pattern

The Abstract Factory Pattern is a Creational Design Pattern that provides an interface for creating families of related or dependent objects without specifying their concrete classes.

Why Do We Need Abstract Factory?

In Factory Method Pattern, we create one object at a time.

However, many applications require creating multiple related objects that belong to the same family.

For example:

  • Windows Button + Windows Checkbox
  • Mac Button + Mac Checkbox
  • Linux Button + Linux Checkbox

We should ensure that related objects belong to the same family.

Problem Without Abstract Factory


Button button =
    new WindowsButton();

Checkbox checkbox =
    new MacCheckbox();

Now the UI contains components from different operating systems.

This creates inconsistency.

Abstract Factory Solution

The Abstract Factory creates an entire family of related objects. 


Client
   |
   v
GUI Factory
   |
   +--> Button
   |
   +--> Checkbox

Depending on the selected factory: 


Windows Factory
    |
    +--> Windows Button
    |
    +--> Windows Checkbox

Mac Factory
    |
    +--> Mac Button
    |
    +--> Mac Checkbox

Implementation

Step 1: Create Product Interfaces


public interface Button {
    void paint();
}

public interface Checkbox {
    void check();
}

Step 2: Create Concrete Products


public class WindowsButton
        implements Button {

    public void paint() {
        System.out.println(
          "Windows Button");
    }
}


public class WindowsCheckbox
        implements Checkbox {

    public void check() {
        System.out.println(
          "Windows Checkbox");
    }
}


public class MacButton
        implements Button {

    public void paint() {
        System.out.println(
          "Mac Button");
    }
}


public class MacCheckbox
        implements Checkbox {

    public void check() {
        System.out.println(
          "Mac Checkbox");
    }
}

Step 3: Create Abstract Factory


public interface GUIFactory {

    Button createButton();

    Checkbox createCheckbox();
}

Step 4: Create Concrete Factories


public class WindowsFactory
        implements GUIFactory {

    public Button createButton() {
        return new WindowsButton();
    }

    public Checkbox createCheckbox() {
        return new WindowsCheckbox();
    }
}


public class MacFactory
        implements GUIFactory {

    public Button createButton() {
        return new MacButton();
    }

    public Checkbox createCheckbox() {
        return new MacCheckbox();
    }
}

Step 5: Client Code


GUIFactory factory =
    new WindowsFactory();

Button button =
    factory.createButton();

Checkbox checkbox =
    factory.createCheckbox();

button.paint();
checkbox.check();

How It Works

  1. Client selects a factory.
  2. Factory creates related products.
  3. Client works with interfaces.
  4. Concrete implementations remain hidden.

Factory Method vs Abstract Factory

Factory Method Abstract Factory
Creates one product Creates product families
Single factory method Multiple factory methods
Simpler More complex
One object creation Group object creation

Real World Example

Imagine a car manufacturing company.

Different factories produce different vehicle families. 


Toyota Factory

Toyota Car
Toyota Engine
Toyota Dashboard


BMW Factory

BMW Car
BMW Engine
BMW Dashboard

The client simply chooses a factory.

Abstract Factory in Spring

Spring's BeanFactory and ApplicationContext are examples of Abstract Factory concepts. 


ApplicationContext context =
    new AnnotationConfigApplicationContext();

UserService service =
    context.getBean(UserService.class);

The framework decides which implementation to provide.

Advantages

  • Ensures compatibility between products.
  • Reduces coupling.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.
  • Centralized object creation.
  • Easy to switch product families.

Disadvantages

  • Many classes and interfaces required.
  • Complex design.
  • Difficult to add new product types.

When to Use

  • Multiple product families exist.
  • Products must work together.
  • Need to switch entire families dynamically.
  • Object creation should be hidden.

When Not to Use

  • Only one product exists.
  • No related object families exist.
  • Simple applications.

Common Interview Questions

What is the difference between Factory Method and Abstract Factory?

Factory Method creates one object. Abstract Factory creates families of related objects.

Which SOLID principle does it support?

Open/Closed Principle and Dependency Inversion Principle.

Where is it used in Spring?

BeanFactory and ApplicationContext follow Abstract Factory concepts.

Interview Quick Notes

  • Abstract Factory creates families of related objects.
  • Client depends on interfaces, not implementations.
  • Ensures product compatibility.
  • More powerful than Factory Method.
  • Commonly used in UI frameworks.
  • Spring BeanFactory is a practical example.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.

subtopics.name: Builder Pattern
subtopics.content:
Builder Pattern

The Builder Pattern is a Creational Design Pattern used to construct complex objects step by step.

It allows the creation of an object with multiple optional parameters while keeping the code readable and maintainable.

Why Do We Need Builder Pattern?

As objects become more complex, constructors often require many parameters.

This leads to the Telescoping Constructor Problem. 


public User(
    String name,
    String email,
    String phone,
    String address,
    String city,
    String state,
    String country
)

Such constructors become difficult to read and maintain.

Builder Pattern solves this problem by constructing objects step by step.

Problem Without Builder Pattern


User user = new User(
    "John",
    "john@gmail.com",
    "9999999999",
    "Street 1",
    "Delhi",
    "Delhi",
    "India"
);

It is difficult to understand which value belongs to which field.

Builder Pattern Solution

Instead of passing all parameters in a constructor, the Builder Pattern allows setting fields one by one. 


User user = User.builder()
                .name("John")
                .email("john@gmail.com")
                .phone("9999999999")
                .city("Delhi")
                .build();

This makes object creation more readable and flexible.

Structure of Builder Pattern


Client
  |
  v
Builder
  |
  v
Product

The Builder creates and configures the Product object.

Implementation

Step 1: Create Product Class


public class User {

    private String name;
    private String email;
    private String phone;

    private User(UserBuilder builder) {
        this.name = builder.name;
        this.email = builder.email;
        this.phone = builder.phone;
    }

    public static UserBuilder builder() {
        return new UserBuilder();
    }

    public static class UserBuilder {

        private String name;
        private String email;
        private String phone;

        public UserBuilder name(String name) {
            this.name = name;
            return this;
        }

        public UserBuilder email(String email) {
            this.email = email;
            return this;
        }

        public UserBuilder phone(String phone) {
            this.phone = phone;
            return this;
        }

        public User build() {
            return new User(this);
        }
    }
}

Step 2: Client Code


User user = User.builder()
        .name("John")
        .email("john@gmail.com")
        .phone("9999999999")
        .build();

How It Works

  1. Client requests a Builder.
  2. Builder sets required and optional fields.
  3. Builder validates data if required.
  4. Builder creates the final object.
  5. Fully configured object is returned.

Real World Example

Consider ordering a custom burger.

Different customers may choose different combinations. 


Burger Builder

+ Cheese
+ Extra Patty
+ Lettuce
+ Onion
+ Sauce

Instead of creating dozens of constructors, a Builder allows flexible configuration.

Builder Pattern with Lombok

Lombok provides built-in support for Builder Pattern. 


@Builder
public class User {

    private String name;
    private String email;
    private String phone;
}

Usage


User user = User.builder()
        .name("John")
        .email("john@gmail.com")
        .build();

Lombok automatically generates the builder code.

Builder vs Constructor

Constructor Builder
Hard to read Highly readable
Limited flexibility Highly flexible
Difficult with many fields Ideal for many fields
Error-prone Less error-prone

Builder Pattern in Spring

ResponseEntity Builder


return ResponseEntity
        .ok()
        .body(user);

UriComponentsBuilder


UriComponentsBuilder
        .fromUriString(url)
        .queryParam("id", 1)
        .build();

Spring Framework heavily uses Builder Pattern.

Advantages

  • Readable object creation.
  • Handles optional fields elegantly.
  • Avoids telescoping constructors.
  • Supports immutable objects.
  • Improves maintainability.
  • Encapsulates construction logic.

Disadvantages

  • Additional code required.
  • More classes and complexity.
  • Not useful for very simple objects.

When to Use

  • Objects contain many fields.
  • Several optional parameters exist.
  • Immutable objects are required.
  • Object creation logic is complex.

When Not to Use

  • Object contains only a few fields.
  • Simple constructor is sufficient.
  • No optional parameters exist.

Common Interview Questions

What problem does Builder Pattern solve?

It solves the Telescoping Constructor Problem and improves readability.

Why is Builder commonly used with immutable classes?

Because all fields can be initialized during object creation and remain unchanged afterward.

Where is Builder Pattern used in Spring?

ResponseEntity, UriComponentsBuilder, WebClient, and many Spring APIs use Builder Pattern.

What Lombok annotation implements Builder Pattern?

@Builder

Interview Quick Notes

  • Builder is a Creational Design Pattern.
  • Used to create complex objects step by step.
  • Solves the Telescoping Constructor Problem.
  • Improves readability and maintainability.
  • Supports immutable object creation.
  • Lombok provides @Builder annotation.
  • Widely used in Spring Framework APIs.

subtopics.name: Prototype Pattern
subtopics.content:
Prototype Pattern

The Prototype Pattern is a Creational Design Pattern that creates new objects by cloning existing objects instead of creating them from scratch.

It is particularly useful when object creation is expensive, time-consuming, or requires complex initialization.

Why Do We Need Prototype Pattern?

Some objects require significant resources to create.

Examples include:

  • Database-loaded objects.
  • Large configuration objects.
  • Game characters.
  • Document templates.
  • Machine learning models.

Creating such objects repeatedly can impact performance.

Instead of creating a new object from scratch, we can clone an existing object.

Problem Without Prototype Pattern


Employee employee = new Employee();

employee.loadDataFromDatabase();

Every time a new Employee object is needed, the application must fetch data and initialize the object again.

This increases execution time and resource usage.

Prototype Pattern Solution

Create one fully initialized object and use it as a prototype.

Whenever a new object is required, clone the prototype instead of recreating it. 


Prototype Object
        |
        v
      Clone
        |
        +--> Object 1
        |
        +--> Object 2
        |
        +--> Object 3

Structure of Prototype Pattern


Client
   |
   v
Prototype Interface
   |
   v
Concrete Prototype
   |
 clone()

Implementation

Step 1: Implement Cloneable


public class Employee
        implements Cloneable {

    private String name;

    public Employee(String name) {
        this.name = name;
    }

    @Override
    protected Employee clone()
            throws CloneNotSupportedException {

        return (Employee) super.clone();
    }
}

Step 2: Clone Object


Employee emp1 =
    new Employee("John");

Employee emp2 =
    emp1.clone();

System.out.println(emp1);
System.out.println(emp2);

Output: 


Different Memory Addresses

Same Data

How It Works

  1. Create a prototype object.
  2. Initialize the prototype.
  3. Clone the prototype whenever needed.
  4. Modify cloned object if required.

Real World Example

Document Templates

Consider a company that generates offer letters.

Instead of creating every document from scratch: 


Offer Letter Template
        |
        v
      Clone
        |
        +--> Employee A
        |
        +--> Employee B
        |
        +--> Employee C

Only employee-specific information changes.

Shallow Copy

Shallow Copy copies primitive fields but shares referenced objects. 


Employee
   |
   +--> Address Object

Both original and cloned objects reference the same Address object.

Example


@Override
protected Employee clone()
        throws CloneNotSupportedException {

    return (Employee) super.clone();
}

Deep Copy

Deep Copy creates copies of referenced objects as well. 


Employee
   |
   +--> Address Copy

Changes in one object do not affect the other.

Example


@Override
protected Employee clone()
        throws CloneNotSupportedException {

    Employee clone =
        (Employee) super.clone();

    clone.address =
        new Address(
            address.getCity()
        );

    return clone;
}

Shallow Copy vs Deep Copy

Feature Shallow Copy Deep Copy
Primitive Fields Copied Copied
Reference Objects Shared Copied
Performance Fast Slower
Safety Lower Higher

Prototype Registry

Large applications often maintain a collection of prototypes. 


Prototype Registry

CAR
BIKE
TRUCK
BUS

Clients request a prototype and receive a cloned object. 


Vehicle vehicle =
    registry.get("CAR").clone();

Cloneable Interface in Java

Java provides the Cloneable interface to support object cloning. 


public class Employee
        implements Cloneable {
}

The clone() method is inherited from Object class.

Prototype vs Builder

Builder Prototype
Creates step-by-step Creates by cloning
Complex construction Fast duplication
New object creation Copy existing object

Prototype Scope in Spring

Spring provides Prototype Bean Scope. 


@Component
@Scope("prototype")
public class Employee {
}

Every request creates a new bean instance. 


Employee e1 =
    context.getBean(Employee.class);

Employee e2 =
    context.getBean(Employee.class);

e1 and e2 are different objects.

Advantages

  • Faster object creation.
  • Reduces initialization cost.
  • Useful for expensive objects.
  • Improves performance.
  • Supports runtime object creation.

Disadvantages

  • Deep copy implementation can be difficult.
  • Cloning complex object graphs is challenging.
  • Cloneable API has design limitations.
  • Maintaining cloned state can become complex.

When to Use

  • Object creation is expensive.
  • Objects are frequently duplicated.
  • Initialization involves database or network calls.
  • Performance optimization is required.

When Not to Use

  • Objects are simple.
  • Object creation cost is negligible.
  • Deep copying introduces unnecessary complexity.

Common Interview Questions

What problem does Prototype Pattern solve?

It avoids expensive object creation by cloning existing objects.

What is the difference between Shallow Copy and Deep Copy?

Shallow Copy shares references while Deep Copy creates independent copies.

Which Java interface supports Prototype Pattern?

Cloneable.

How is Prototype used in Spring?

Through Prototype Bean Scope using @Scope("prototype").

Interview Quick Notes

  • Prototype is a Creational Design Pattern.
  • Creates objects by cloning existing objects.
  • Useful when object creation is expensive.
  • Supports Shallow Copy and Deep Copy.
  • Cloneable interface enables cloning in Java.
  • Spring supports Prototype Scope.
  • Improves performance for frequently duplicated objects.

subtopics.name: Adapter Pattern
subtopics.content:
Adapter Pattern

The Adapter Pattern is a Structural Design Pattern that allows two incompatible interfaces to work together.

It acts as a bridge between an existing class and a client that expects a different interface.

Why Do We Need Adapter Pattern?

In real-world applications, we often need to integrate third-party libraries, legacy systems, or external APIs whose interfaces do not match our application's requirements.

Instead of modifying existing code, we create an Adapter that translates one interface into another.

Real World Example

Mobile Charger Adapter

A mobile charger cannot be plugged directly into a high-voltage power source. 


Power Socket
      |
      v
   Adapter
      |
      v
Mobile Charger

The adapter converts the interface and voltage into a compatible format.

Similarly, the Adapter Pattern converts one software interface into another.

Problem Without Adapter Pattern

Suppose our application expects JSON notifications. 


NotificationService
       |
       v
JSON Notification

A third-party library provides XML notifications. 


Third Party Service
       |
       v
XML Notification

The interfaces are incompatible.

Adapter Pattern Solution

Create an Adapter that converts XML into JSON. 


Client
   |
   v
Adapter
   |
   v
Third Party Service

The client works with its expected interface while the adapter handles conversion.

Structure of Adapter Pattern


Client
   |
Target Interface
   |
Adapter
   |
Adaptee

Where:

  • Client uses Target Interface.
  • Adapter implements Target Interface.
  • Adaptee contains existing incompatible code.

Implementation

Step 1: Target Interface


public interface PaymentProcessor {

    void processPayment();
}

Step 2: Existing Incompatible Class


public class LegacyPaymentSystem {

    public void makePayment() {

        System.out.println(
            "Payment Processed"
        );
    }
}

Step 3: Create Adapter


public class PaymentAdapter
        implements PaymentProcessor {

    private LegacyPaymentSystem
            legacyPaymentSystem;

    public PaymentAdapter(
        LegacyPaymentSystem
            legacyPaymentSystem) {

        this.legacyPaymentSystem =
                legacyPaymentSystem;
    }

    @Override
    public void processPayment() {

        legacyPaymentSystem
            .makePayment();
    }
}

Step 4: Client Code


PaymentProcessor processor =
    new PaymentAdapter(
        new LegacyPaymentSystem()
    );

processor.processPayment();

How It Works

  1. Client calls the Target Interface.
  2. Adapter receives the request.
  3. Adapter converts the request.
  4. Adapter invokes the Adaptee.
  5. Response is returned to the Client.

Class Diagram


                 Client
                    |
                    v
          PaymentProcessor
                    ^
                    |
             PaymentAdapter
                    |
                    v
        LegacyPaymentSystem

Object Adapter

Object Adapter uses composition. 


Adapter HAS-A Adaptee

This is the most commonly used implementation.

Advantages

  • Flexible.
  • Supports multiple adaptees.
  • Follows composition over inheritance.

Class Adapter

Class Adapter uses inheritance. 


Adapter EXTENDS Adaptee

Java does not support multiple class inheritance, so this approach is less common.

Adapter vs Facade

Adapter Facade
Makes incompatible interfaces compatible Simplifies complex interfaces
Focuses on compatibility Focuses on simplification
Wraps one object May wrap multiple subsystems

Adapter vs Decorator

Adapter Decorator
Changes interface Adds behavior
Solves compatibility issues Enhances functionality

Adapter Pattern in Spring Framework

HandlerAdapter

Spring MVC uses HandlerAdapter to allow different controller implementations to work through a common interface. 


DispatcherServlet
        |
        v
   HandlerAdapter
        |
        v
Controller

The DispatcherServlet does not need to know the exact controller type.

JdbcTemplate

JdbcTemplate internally adapts low-level JDBC APIs into simpler operations.

Real Life Use Cases

  • Third-party API integration.
  • Legacy system integration.
  • Payment gateway integration.
  • Database driver adaptation.
  • XML to JSON conversion.
  • Spring MVC HandlerAdapter.

Advantages

  • Promotes code reusability.
  • Integrates legacy systems easily.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.
  • Avoids modifying existing code.
  • Improves flexibility.

Disadvantages

  • Additional abstraction layer.
  • Increases complexity.
  • Too many adapters can make code difficult to maintain.

When to Use

  • Integrating third-party libraries.
  • Working with legacy code.
  • Interfaces are incompatible.
  • Modification of existing code is not possible.

When Not to Use

  • Interfaces are already compatible.
  • The adaptee can be modified directly.
  • Additional abstraction is unnecessary.

Common Interview Questions

What problem does Adapter Pattern solve?

It allows incompatible interfaces to work together.

What is the difference between Adapter and Facade?

Adapter focuses on compatibility while Facade focuses on simplification.

What is the difference between Adapter and Decorator?

Adapter changes an interface, whereas Decorator adds functionality.

Where is Adapter Pattern used in Spring?

HandlerAdapter in Spring MVC is a classic example.

Interview Quick Notes

  • Adapter is a Structural Design Pattern.
  • It converts one interface into another.
  • Used to integrate incompatible systems.
  • Object Adapter uses composition.
  • Class Adapter uses inheritance.
  • Spring MVC HandlerAdapter is a common example.
  • Supports Open/Closed Principle.
  • Frequently used in third-party integrations.

subtopics.name: Bridge Pattern
subtopics.content:
Bridge Pattern

The Bridge Pattern is a Structural Design Pattern that separates an abstraction from its implementation so that both can evolve independently.

Instead of tightly coupling abstraction and implementation through inheritance, the Bridge Pattern uses composition.

Why Do We Need Bridge Pattern?

As applications grow, inheritance hierarchies can become very large and difficult to maintain.

Consider a Remote Control application.

We have:

  • Basic Remote
  • Advanced Remote

And devices:

  • TV
  • Radio
  • Speaker

Without Bridge Pattern: 


BasicTVRemote
AdvancedTVRemote

BasicRadioRemote
AdvancedRadioRemote

BasicSpeakerRemote
AdvancedSpeakerRemote

The number of classes keeps growing as new remotes and devices are added.

Problem Without Bridge Pattern


              Remote
                 |
        -----------------
        |               |
      TV             Radio
        |               |
   AdvancedTV     AdvancedRadio

This creates a class explosion problem.

Every new device requires new remote implementations.

Bridge Pattern Solution

Separate abstraction (Remote) from implementation (Device). 


Remote
   |
   +--------> Device

Now both hierarchies can evolve independently.

Real World Example

Remote and Devices


Basic Remote
Advanced Remote
       |
       v
      Device
       |
  +----+----+
  |         |
 TV      Radio

The remote works with any device through a common interface.

Structure of Bridge Pattern


Abstraction
    |
Refined Abstraction
    |
    v
Implementation Interface
    |
Concrete Implementations

Implementation

Step 1: Create Implementation Interface


public interface Device {

    void turnOn();

    void turnOff();

    void setVolume(int volume);
}

Step 2: Create Concrete Implementations


public class TV
        implements Device {

    @Override
    public void turnOn() {
        System.out.println("TV ON");
    }

    @Override
    public void turnOff() {
        System.out.println("TV OFF");
    }

    @Override
    public void setVolume(int volume) {
        System.out.println(
            "TV Volume: " + volume
        );
    }
}


public class Radio
        implements Device {

    @Override
    public void turnOn() {
        System.out.println("Radio ON");
    }

    @Override
    public void turnOff() {
        System.out.println("Radio OFF");
    }

    @Override
    public void setVolume(int volume) {
        System.out.println(
            "Radio Volume: " + volume
        );
    }
}

Step 3: Create Abstraction


public abstract class Remote {

    protected Device device;

    public Remote(Device device) {
        this.device = device;
    }

    public abstract void power();
}

Step 4: Create Refined Abstraction


public class BasicRemote
        extends Remote {

    public BasicRemote(Device device) {
        super(device);
    }

    @Override
    public void power() {

        device.turnOn();
    }
}


public class AdvancedRemote
        extends Remote {

    public AdvancedRemote(
            Device device) {

        super(device);
    }

    @Override
    public void power() {

        device.turnOn();
    }

    public void mute() {

        device.setVolume(0);
    }
}
